coredata.dc:title: An electrothermal coupling model for estimating the internal temperature of lithium-ion battery based on microthermal resistance method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Ying
Zhang, Ying
Zeng, Min
Huang, Xingyuan
Wang, Zhiqiang
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, the CN-CD30V10A battery tester, K-type temperature sensor, and RS485 are utilized to perform charging and discharging tests on the Panasonic 21,700 lithium-ion battery at various rates. The voltage and temperature changes are measured in real-time, with the data transmitted to the computer via RS485. Then a micro-thermal resistance method is proposed to calculate the equivalent thermal resistance of the cell core along the axial positive and negative poles, as well as along the radial outer circumference surface under anisotropic conditions. Based on the experimental results and the equivalent thermal resistance, an electrothermal coupling model is established for battery internal temperature estimation, taking into account anisotropy and temperature effects on heat transfer. Through the integration of the model and externally measured temperature, the internal temperature is accurately estimated. Moreover, the proposed model (Root-Mean-Square Error, RMSE=0.329) exhibits a significant improvement in accuracy compared to equivalent circuit model (RMSE=1.08), extended Kalman filter (RMSE=0.95), network extended Kalman filter (RMSE=0.58), and neural network unscented Kalman filter (RMSE=0.61). Specifically, the model achieves an approximately 76 % to 228 % reduction in RMSE compared to these methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micro-thermal resistance method
Heat transfer
Lithium-ion battery
Electrothermal coupling model
Internal temperature estimation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Battery geometry and temperature measurement location: (a) Schematic diagram; (b) A photograph of the experimental test.
Temperature variations at distinct positions under varying charge and discharge rates: (a) 1.3C charging rate; (b) 1C discharging rate; (c) 1.5C discharging rate; (d) 2C discharging rate.
Schematic representation of thermal resistance grid partitioning: (a) Battery axial thermal resistance grid; (b) Battery radial thermal resistance grid.
The flow chart of battery internal temperature estimation.
Temperature distribution at various battery positions under different discharge rates (experiment vs. simulation): (a) Positive column; (b) Outer circumferential surface; (c) Cathode position.
External and internal temperature under different discharge rate: (a) 1C discharge rate; (b) 1.5C discharge rate; (c) 2C discharge rate.

row.entry: The third type of boundary condition
The third type of boundary condition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The RMSE between the simulated and measured battery temperature.
Results estimated in this paper vs. experimental findings reported by Du et al.[30].
Comparative analysis of internal temperature accuracy across different methodologies.

head.ce:title: An electrothermal coupling model for estimating the internal temperature of lithium-ion battery based on microthermal resistance method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Manufacturing Institute, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Manufacturing Institute, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Nanchang Institute of Science and Technology, Nanchang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering
Nanchang Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Nanchang Institute of Technology, Nanchang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, the CN-CD30V10A battery tester, K-type temperature sensor, and RS485 are utilized to perform charging and discharging tests on the Panasonic 21,700 lithium-ion battery at various rates. The voltage and temperature changes are measured in real-time, with the data transmitted to the computer via RS485. Then a micro-thermal resistance method is proposed to calculate the equivalent thermal resistance of the cell core along the axial positive and negative poles, as well as along the radial outer circumference surface under anisotropic conditions. Based on the experimental results and the equivalent thermal resistance, an electrothermal coupling model is established for battery internal temperature estimation, taking into account anisotropy and temperature effects on heat transfer. Through the integration of the model and externally measured temperature, the internal temperature is accurately estimated. Moreover, the proposed model (Root-Mean-Square Error, RMSE=0.329) exhibits a significant improvement in accuracy compared to equivalent circuit model (RMSE=1.08), extended Kalman filter (RMSE=0.95), network extended Kalman filter (RMSE=0.58), and neural network unscented Kalman filter (RMSE=0.61). Specifically, the model achieves an approximately 76 % to 228 % reduction in RMSE compared to these methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the global new energy vehicle industry has witnessed remarkable growth, with lithium-ion batteries emerging as the predominant power source due to their exceptional energy density and extended cycle life [1,2]. As the utilization of lithium-ion batteries continues to expand in the automotive and power grid energy storage sectors, customers are increasingly prioritizing battery capacity and charging efficiency [3]. During the process of charging and discharging a battery pack, chemical and electrochemical reactions occur, generating a substantial amount of heat. The excessive heat has detrimental effects on battery performance, hastens battery aging, and potentially triggers a thermal runaway event, which in extreme cases may result in fire incidents [4]. Therefore, efficient thermal management plays a pivotal role in onboard applications [5], with battery temperature estimation posing a critical concern. The surface temperature of the battery had been widely employed as a target parameter for battery thermal management in numerous studies, owing to its ease of acquisition. In some cases, simplified calculation methods also had been employed by adopting reduced heat models [6–9], which utilized the volume (or mass) average temperature as a singular parameter to assess the battery's thermal dynamic performance. However, the absence of real-time monitoring for high temperatures within the battery may potentially lead to thermal runaway in these approaches. Zhang et al. [10] demonstrated that there could be a temperature difference exceeding 10℃ between the interior and exterior of the battery during high-speed charging and discharging processes. Therefore, continuous monitoring of internal temperature is crucial to ensure safety performance, longevity, and mitigate the risk of thermal runaway [11].

Currently, extensive scholarly researches had been conducted on the topic of internal temperature monitoring in batteries. Several researchers [12–17] had employed direct measurement techniques. Yu et al. [12] integrated fiber optic sensors into the battery core position, enabling precise temperature monitoring. Mutyala et al. [13] utilized a convertible flexible film thermocouple to accurately measure the internal temperature. Zhang et al. [14] incorporated nickel foil elements as self-heating temperature sensors within the battery. Wang et al. [15] integrated a commercially available PT1000 sensor into a lithium button battery for real-time monitoring of internal temperature. Zhang et al. [16] positioned T-type thermocouples inside and on the surface of both positive and negative terminals to measure both internal and surface temperatures simultaneously. Worwood et al. [17] implemented an axial heat pipe within the battery to achieve effective thermal management.

Despite the simplicity and effectiveness of direct measurement methods, the placement of temperature sensors within the battery inevitably exerts a certain impact on its structure, potentially compromising battery performance, hastening aging processes, and diminishing safety margins. Consequently, numerous scholars had shifted their focus towards investigating indirect monitoring approaches. Among them, the electrochemical model method [18,19] had been widely employed due to its capability in calculating the temperature distribution of batteries. Liu et al. [18] developed a circuit and electrochemical coupling model for a single-layer lithium-ion battery using the finite element method, which was further extended to a three-dimensional battery model based on the specific battery structure. Wang et al. [19] proposed a comprehensive electrochemical model, along with a decoupling estimation method to accurately determine electrochemical and thermal-related parameters, which was validated through an embedded thermocouple experimental approach to assess its accuracy.

Despite its good estimation accuracy, the electrochemical model is deemed unsuitable for online applications due to its complexity and the cumbersome nature of the required parameters. Some scholars had also employed the impedance spectroscopy method [17,20–22], wherein an impedance spectroscopy meter was primarily utilized to measure the battery's impedance spectrum, enabling estimation of the internal temperature based on the correlation between battery internal resistance and temperature. However, as the battery's internal resistance is influenced not only by temperature but also by the state of charge (SOC) and state of health (SOH), a comprehensive series of tests is necessary to determine the frequency interval that minimizes the impact of SOC and SOH. Subsequently, an appropriate frequency corresponding to the impedance spectrum can be selected for temperature estimation in order to achieve more precise results. Furthermore, impedance spectroscopy meters are relatively costly and challenging to widely promote. The data-driven approach had been extensively employed in various research domains owing to its rapid data processing capabilities, and some researchers had also utilized this technique for estimating the internal temperature of batteries [23,24]. However, a prerequisite for employing data-driven methods is the availability of a substantial amount of representative data, which can be arduous and time-consuming to obtain.

The integration of an appropriate battery model with the Kalman filter algorithm presents a highly effective approach for investigating battery thermal characteristics [25–29]. Hui et al. [25] employed a combination of an equivalent circuit model and a dual-state thermal model to establish an enhanced electro-thermal model, while Shi et al. [26] developed a lumped parameter model. Zhang et al. [27] developed an electro-thermal coupling model based on the battery equivalent circuit, Ma et al. [28] established a reduced-order thermal model, and Sun et al. [29] proposed a simplified battery thermal balance model for quantitative analysis of the battery's heat generation characteristics. Subsequently, these scholars utilized established models and the Kalman filter algorithm to estimate the internal temperature. Furthermore, other researchers conducted related investigations employing diverse methodologies. Du et al. [30] devised an experimental system for monitoring the average internal temperature using infrared techniques, however, this approach lacks universality. Wang et al. [31] proposed a simplified distributed electro-thermal model for cylindrical lithium-ion batteries. Pablo et al. [32] estimated the surface and internal temperature using a dual-state estimation method based on the equivalent circuit electro-thermal model. Lin et al. [33] designed an internal temperature adaptive observer based on online parameterization methods and surface temperature measurement.

The majority of literature had relied on battery thermal models to estimate internal temperature, but these models often made isotropic assumptions or only consider radial heat transfer, neglecting the influence of axial heat transfer on battery internal temperature. Additionally, none of the aforementioned studies have accounted for the impact of surface heat transfer on temperature.

The micro thermal resistance method is employed in this article to estimate the internal temperature of the battery. Specifically, the battery is discretized into micro-element bodies along both radial and axial directions, allowing for calculation of their respective thermal resistances. These individual thermal resistances are then interconnected in a series–parallel configuration to form a thermal grid, facilitating estimation of the internal temperature based on real-time measurements of the surface temperature. A simulation model is developed using Simscape software, which comprehensively accounts for heat generation and transfer mechanisms while considering the anisotropic nature of thermal conductivity within the battery and temperature impacst on surface heat transfer.

The CN-CD30V10A battery tester is employed for conducting constant current discharge tests on Panasonic 21,700 lithium-ion batteries, while simultaneously monitoring the voltage fluctuations throughout the discharge process. Additionally, RS485 and K-type thermocouples are utilized for temperature monitoring at various positions during the discharge process. Under an ambient temperature of 20℃, the batteries undergo constant current discharge at rates of 1C, 1.5C, and 2C while utilizing K-type thermocouples to monitor temperatures at three distinct positions along the battery's circumference as well as the positive and negative terminals. The cell's geometric parameters and distribution positions of the thermocouples are illustrated in Fig. 1 . To mitigate potential interference between the thermocouple data transmission signal and the battery charging and discharging signal, ultra-fine K-type thermocouples with Kempson welding pin type for positive pole and thin film pin type for negative pole are utilized. The diameter of the welding point for Kempson welding pin type thermocouple is less than 0.3 mm, while that of thin film pin type is merely 0.1 mm thick. Three uniformly distributed Kempson welding pin type thermocouples are positioned on the surface. Real-time temperature data is acquired using an RS485 temperature acquisition module and communication converter, which is subsequently transmitted to a computer for analysis.

The battery experiment employs a constant current-constant voltage charging and discharging mode, wherein the charging current rapidly decreases when the lithium-ion battery enters the constant voltage charging phase, resulting in a significant extension of the battery's charging duration. Consequently, this study focuses on analyzing the state of charge within the range of 0.2 to 0.8.

During the experiment, a charging rate of 1.3C is maintained, while discharge rates of 1C, 1.5C, and 2C are utilized. To minimize potential measurement errors inherent in the experimental procedure, multiple measurements are conducted and a data fusion technique is implemented to mitigate any measurement noise present. The temperature data obtained from various locations on the battery surface under different charge and discharge rates are depicted in Fig. 2 .

The temperature measurement results of the aforementioned charging and discharging ratios exhibit a consistent and analogous trend across various locations surrounding the battery throughout the entire charging and discharging process, particularly at T2, T3, and T4. Furthermore, the observed temperature discrepancy at each time point remains relatively negligible within the measurement accuracy range of ± 0.5℃. Consequently, for subsequent modeling purposes in the following section, it is assumed that there exists uniformity in surface temperature along the outer circumference of the battery.

At a charging rate of 1.3C, the battery undergoes an initial constant current charging phase with a magnitude of 6A. After approximately 1200 s, the battery reaches its maximum charging voltage and transitions into a constant voltage charging mode. Throughout the entire charging process, there is an initial rapid temperature rise at each position due to significant heat generation by the battery. However, as the charging progresses, the temperature difference between the battery and its surroundings increases, leading to enhanced heat dissipation and gradual reduction in accumulated heat. Consequently, the rate of temperature growth decelerates. When applying constant voltage during battery charging, there is a sharp decline in internal heat generation within the battery resulting in a downward trend observed in its temperature.

Under varying charging and discharging rates, the initial heat generation leads to a rapid temperature rise in both the positive and negative electrodes, with the positive electrode exhibiting a higher heating rate. Throughout the charge and discharge process, the temperature of the positive electrode consistently remains higher than that of other locations. At the conclusion of charge and discharge cycles, the experimental measurements indicate a common trend where T1 > T2≈T3≈T4 > T5.

Based on the experimental results, the following conclusions can be inferred: (1) The temperature variations across different positions on the circumferential surface exhibit consistency throughout the entire discharge process; (2) The heat conduction rate along the axial direction from battery core to positive pole is maximized, while it is minimized along the radial direction. Consequently, during early stages of high heat generation within the battery, both positive and negative poles tend to exhibit higher temperatures compared to those observed on the circumferential surface; (3) The natural convection heat transfer of the battery's positive and negative terminals surpasses that of its outer surface. As discharge progresses, enhanced heat dissipation in both positive and negative electrodes mitigates temperature rise. In fact, at times, even lower temperatures may be observed at the negative terminal compared to those recorded on its circumference.

The electrical model employed in this article is an equivalent circuit model. The physical representation is depicted in Fig. 3 , wherein the current passing through
R 0

are determined based on the offline parameter identification results obtained from the author's previous research paper [34]. Meanwhile, I represents the charging and discharging current.

represents battery capacity and I represents excitation current.

Based on the experimental findings presented in the preceding section, fundamental assumptions are established:
(1)
The battery exhibits anisotropy along its positive and negative poles, as well as radial heat conduction;
(2)
The temperature distribution across the battery's outer circumference is uniform;
(3)
The highest temperature within the battery is located at its center.

Due to the anisotropic characteristics of heat transfer in the battery, specifically the inconsistent axial and radial thermal conductivity, this section employs the battery thermal resistance grid method to programmatically calculate the equivalent resistance in three directions: radial, positive pole axial, and negative pole axial.

The axial equivalent thermal resistance is calculated through the following steps: (1) The circular section is divided into n equal parts along the radial direction, where adjacent annuli have a radius difference of
dr
and an area of
d
A i
per annulus; (2) The thermal resistance
R i
between the battery core and each annulus is determined based on the axial thermal conductivity; (3) The
R i
values are connected in parallel to calculate the effective thermal resistance
R h
for heat transfer along the axis of the battery core.

The calculation of radial equivalent thermal resistance involves the following steps: (1) Divide the outer circumference into m equal parts along the height direction, with each ring having an area denoted as
d
A j
; (2) Calculate the thermal resistance
R j
between the battery core and each micro-surface based on the radial thermal conductivity; (3) Parallelize
R j
and calculate
R r
, which represents the radial heat transfer equivalent thermal resistance.
The grid division of thermal resistance is illustrated in Fig. 4 .

The procedure for calculating the axial equivalent thermal resistance is as follows:

In which,

represents the distance from the i-th elemental volume to the core, h represents the height, n represents the number of divided grids, r represents the radius,
λ h
denotes the axial thermal conductivity.

where,
δ j
represents the distance from the j-th elemental volume to the core, h represents the height, m represents the number of grid divisions, r represents the radius, and
λ r
represents the radial thermal conductivity.

The battery thermal model in this article is predicated on the following fundamental assumptions:
(1)
Heat generation within the battery primarily occurs at its core;
(2)
The cylindrical battery exhibits radial and axial anisotropy, while maintaining uniform thermal conductivity.

The total heat generation is determined by employing the Bernadi equation,

The heat transfer grid is illustrated in Fig. 5 , wherein q denotes the heat flow generated by the battery,
q 2

R 5

The internal temperature is estimated in this section using the established electric-thermal grid coupling model, considering a temperature condition of 20℃. Firstly, the model is initialized with identical initial and boundary conditions as those of the experiment, while the discharge rates are set at 1C, 1.5C, and 2C respectively. Subsequently, temperature measurements are conducted at various positions on the battery surface to validate the model against experimental results. Furthermore, utilizing the established model, an estimation of internal battery temperature is performed. The simulation flow chart is presented in Fig. 6 .
The initial and boundary conditions are described in Table 1 .

The simulation analysis is conducted under different discharge rates, namely 1C, 1.5C, and 2C. Simultaneously, the temperature measurements are taken at the outer circumferential surface and positive/negative pole positions of the battery, which are then compared with corresponding experimental results obtained under identical conditions. The experimentally observed and simulated outcomes for various discharge rates are presented in Fig. 7 .

The analysis of Fig. 7a reveals that, across various discharge rates, there exists an approximate temperature difference of 1℃ between the simulated and experimental results at the initial discharge time for the positive electrode position. Furthermore, as the discharge progresses, this disparity gradually diminishes; According to Fig. 7b, the simulation results of the outer circumferential surface temperature exhibit excellent agreement with the experimental findings across various discharge rates. Furthermore, the simulation outcomes for battery negative column temperature at both 1C and 2C rate discharges demonstrate remarkable consistency with the corresponding experimental results according to Fig. 7c.

In order to further investigate the consistency between the thermal characteristics of the battery model and real-world batteries, Table 2 presents the root-mean-square error (RMSE) between simulated temperature values and measured values. Additionally, we compare our model with that proposed by Lin et al.[35].

The battery in Lin's paper is modeled as a homogeneous body with uniform temperature, neglecting anisotropy. The RMSE between the calculated battery surface temperature using this model and the experimental results is 0.65. In contrast, our proposed model exhibits lower RMSE values when comparing the calculated temperatures with experimental results at different discharge rates, specifically achieving RMSE values within 0.3 for discharge rates of 1C and 2C.

In conclusion, by considering the influence of anisotropy and temperature on convective heat transfer, the battery electrothermal coupling model based on the micro-thermal resistance method exhibits enhanced capability in simulating the thermal characteristics of real batteries.

Considering that the internal heat of a battery can solely be transferred to its outer surface through heat conduction, followed by dissipation via specific thermal management techniques, it is inevitable for the internal temperature to surpass the surface temperature during high-temperature and high-rate discharges. Consequently, this premature rise in internal temperature beyond the optimal range may lead to performance degradation and even fire hazards. However, due to the difficulty in directly measuring the internal temperature, it is crucial to estimate this temperature based on both the measured surface temperature and thermal characteristics of the battery itself. In this section, we present estimated internal temperatures under different discharge rates at an ambient temperature of 20 ℃ using the flow chart shown in Fig. 6, as depicted in Fig. 8 .

According to Fig. 8, the internal temperature exceeds that of the outer position at varying discharge rates due to the necessity for heat conduction to transfer internal heat towards the outer surface, followed by its dissipation through appropriate thermal management. The temperature of the positive column closely approximates the internal temperature when the discharge rate is low, as depicted in Fig. 8a. The maximum difference between them is merely 0.1 ℃, suggesting that measuring the temperature of the positive column at a low discharge rate can serve as an estimation for the internal temperature. Furthermore, Fig. 8b and 8c reveal a gradual increase in disparity between the internal and external temperatures with an escalation in discharge rate. At a discharge rate of 1.5C, there is a temperature difference of 0.6 ℃ between internal and positive, while under condition where battery discharge at a rate of 2C, this difference can increase to nearly 2 ℃ exhibiting noticeable non-uniformity across different positions.

In order to validate the accuracy of internal temperature estimation in this study, a comparison between the estimated internal temperatures at a discharge rate of 1C in this study and the measured values reported by Du et al.[30] under identical discharge conditions is presented in Table 3 .

The simulation results in this study, conducted at a discharge rate of 1C, demonstrate excellent agreement with the experimental findings reported by Du et al., as presented in Table 3. Consequently, it can be inferred that the proposed internal temperature estimation outcomes in this paper exhibit high reliability.

By employing the experimental findings of Du et al. as reference values, we calculate the RMSE of the simulation results for internal temperature in this study. Subsequently, a comparative analysis is conducted with various methodologies documented in existing literature, including the equivalent circuit model (ECM), extended Kalman filter (EKF), neural network extended Kalman filter (NNEKF), and neural network unscented Kalman filter (NNUKF). The outcomes of this comparison are presented in Table 4 .

The estimated results obtained by the ECM and EKF methods mentioned in Liu et al. exhibit RMSE values of 1.08 and 0.95, respectively, as shown in Table 4. Liu et al.'s study proposes the NNEKF method with an RMSE of 0.58, while Lin et al.'s approach yields an RMSE of 0.61 for the estimated results. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [27] have highlighted that most literature-based methods achieve estimation accuracies ranging from 0.5 to 2. In this paper, we employ the microresistance method to calculate isotropic equivalent thermal resistance and establish an internal temperature estimation model considering the influence of temperature on convective heat transfer's thermal resistance. Under this model, we achieve a significantly improved RMSE value of 0.329.

Despite the extensive research conducted on battery internal temperature estimation, the majority of studies have overlooked the anisotropic nature of heat conduction within batteries. Only a limited number of papers have considered two-dimensional anisotropy along radial and transverse directions, while none have addressed the variation in heat transfer coefficient on the battery surface during modeling.

In the current work, the voltage and temperature fluctuations in real-time during the process of charging and discharging are recorded by experiment. Subsequently, the innovative micro-thermal resistance method is employed to determine the thermal resistance from the core position of the battery to its positive and negative columns as well as its surface circumference. Therefore, an innovative electrothermal coupling model for internal temperature estimation is developed based on experimental results and equivalent thermal resistance, incorporating the influence of temperature on its surface convective heat transfer coefficient. This approach yields the following significant findings:
(1)
A thermoelectric coupling model is developed to accurately estimate the internal temperature of the battery, taking into account the impact of anisotropy and temperature on its heat transfer characteristics. The simulation results of the model are compared with the experiment results under identical conditions, revealing RMSE values for outer surface temperature of 0.248, 0.545 and 0.267 at discharge rates of 1C, 1.5C and 2C respectively. In addition, the model presented in this study demonstrates accurate simulation of the thermal behavior of real batteries, as compared to Lin et al.'s simulation without considering anisotropy (RMSE=0.65).
(2)
Utilizing this model for simulating the internal battery temperature, it is observed that at a discharge rate of 1C, there exists a minimum temperature difference of 0.1 °C between the positive electrode and the internal battery temperature, thereby validating the efficacy of employing positive electrode measurements as an alternative to direct internal temperature readings. The temperature difference between the internal and maximum external temperatures at discharge completion is approximately 0.6℃ and 2℃ when the discharge rate is increased to 1.5C and 2C, respectively.
(3)
The predicted results obtained by this method exhibit a high level of consistency with the experimental findings of Du et al., under identical conditions of ambient temperature and discharge rate, thereby validating the reliability of the model proposed in the current work.
(4)
The proposed model (RMSE=0.329) exhibit a significant reduction in comparison to ECM (RMSE=1.08), EKF (RMSE=0.95), NNEKF (RMSE=0.58), and NNUKF (RMSE=0.61). The RMSE of this model is reduced by 76 % to 228 %, when compared with these methods, further substantiating the precision of this model.
Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Light Alloy, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China.

Ying Xu: Software, Methodology. Ying Zhang: Software, Methodology. Min Zeng: Writing – review & editing. Xingyuan Huang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Conceptualization. Zhiqiang Wang: Visualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been supported by the National Regional Science Foundation of China (Grant No.51863014) and Science and Technology Research Project in Jiangxi Province and Department of Education (Nos.GJJ191111 and GJJ2202905)
This work was supported by the Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Light Alloy (2024SSY05031).
